narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 40 (above the average 36.70), Year 1 school decile of 2 (well below the average 5.71), Year 3 decile of 1 (much lower than the average 5.53), upper-middle family income, and full-time student status—and predicted a 47.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their program. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by standing out as stronger than most applicants'. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking was the most decisive drag, strongly signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar failure due to its low position compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile also contributed toward the failure prediction, though with less weight, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance relative to the typical student. Overall, while your LSAT and solid family background offered some counterbalance, the model weighed your weaker grade deciles—especially in later years—as the dominant signals of risk, setting you apart from applicants with consistently higher school rankings who typically see lower failure predictions. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment highlighting where your record diverged from successful bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to build on your strength there, or focus on improving undergraduate or graduate grades to boost your decile rankings in future applications. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources or advising from programs that support Asian students navigating competitive legal admissions. With strategic steps like these, you can reshape your profile for stronger odds next time.
